title: Punching Way Above Its Weight: My Models Score Big on Intel's Low-Bit LLM Leaderboard (Needs some editing)
summary: I checked the Intel Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard and found that several of my recent models are performing far above expectations—including a 4B parameter distilled Claude model that ranks third overall, 1.2B models that beat popular 1B baselines, and even a 0.2B tiny model that outperforms OpenAI's 20B parameter GPT-oss-20B. Here's the full breakdown of each model and its results.
content:
I Was Checking the Leaderboard—and Got Some Surprises

I was just browsing the Intel Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard and noticed something pretty wild: a bunch of my recent models are ranking way higher than I expected, some against models that are dozens of times larger. Let's break down what each one does, how they performed, and why these results matter.

The Current Leaderboard Snapshots

Here's how the top of the field looks for the nearby models, with mine highlighted in orange:

RankModelAverage Score (%)
1Qwen3-4B (W4A16)64.1%
2Qwen3-4B-Thinking (W4A16)63.4%
3FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (NVFP4-RTN)62.7%
4FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (MXFP4-RTN)61.2%
5Qwen3-4B (MXFP4)60.0%
6MiniCPM5-1B (MXFP4)47.5%
7FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (W4A16-RTN)46.8%
8Qwen3.5-0.8B (NVFP4)46.2%
9FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX (W4A16-RTN)45.2%
10Qwen3-0.6B (W4A16)44.3%
15GPT-oss-20B (W4A16)36.0%
16XiaomiMiMo-MiMo-V2.5-ASR34.6%
17FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt (W4A16-RTN)34.4%
18FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it (W4A16-RTN)34.2%
19FlameF0X/LFM2-Research (W4A16-Tuning)34.0%

That's some serious competition—we're sitting right next to big names like MiniCPM5-1B and even beating OpenAI's 20B parameter GPT-oss-20B with models that are 100× smaller.

The Star Player: Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

This is probably my most capable release yet: a 4-billion parameter multimodal model distilled directly from Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (the claude.ai version, not the API variant). The whole point was to see how much of Claude's reasoning style and conversational behavior I could squeeze into something you can still reasonably run on local hardware.

The two low-bit variants—NVFP4 and MXFP4—both land above 61% average on the Intel leaderboard, sitting right under the base Qwen3-4B and its Thinking variant, and even ahead of the base Qwen3-4B MXFP4 quantization. Under the hood it's a chat-optimized, multimodal Qwen3-4B that plays nicely with Transformers (via AutoModelForMultimodalLM), vLLM, Docker, and SGLang.

If you just want to spin it up and poke at it, the model card has ready-made snippets for the pipeline API, direct AutoTokenizer/AutoModelForMultimodalLM usage, and OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoints. You can grab it here: FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6.

Compact Reasoning & Coding: The LFM2.5-1.2B Lineup

These two are my 1.2B-scale Liquid AI experiments you might've seen earlier, but it's fun seeing them in context on a low-bit leaderboard.

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — "Liquid Claude". This is a distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking onto LiquidAI/LFM2.5-1.2B-Base. It's Apache 2.0, BF16 weights, and trained on a private dataset of Claude conversations with a heavy focus on chain-of-thought and tool-style thinking blocks. On Intel's leaderboard it scores an average of 46.76% (HellaSwag 39.51, MMLU 31.99, PIQA 68.77), which turns into 46.8% in the low-bit bar chart—comfortably ahead of MiniCPM5-1B.
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — "Liquid CodeX". Same LFM2.5 backbone, but instead of pure Claude distillation it's tuned on Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking: about 2 million code examples with explicit reasoning traces. It targets coding and technical reasoning. On Intel's leaderboard it hits a 45.25% average (HellaSwag 39.70, MMLU 26.56, PIQA 69.48), which shows up as 45.2% in the chart—again, right in the mix with the best sub-1B Qwen models.

Both are Apache 2.0, come with quantized variants for llama.cpp / LM Studio / Jan / Ollama, and plug straight into Transformers or vLLM. If you want a tiny open model with Claude-style thinking baked in, these are basically my go-to recommendations:

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (Liquid Claude)
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX (Liquid CodeX)

Tiny Models That Beat Giants: 0.2B Qwen2 Variants

Honestly, these might be the funniest part of the chart: two 0.2B models that edge out a 20B one.

The pair:

  • Qwen2-0.2B-pt — a 200M parameter pretrain built on Qwen2, trained on a mix of Salesforce/wikitext, roneneldan/TinyStories, and Skylion007/openwebtext. It's a straight text-generation LM (no instructions) and ships as FP32 with Apache 2.0. The card focuses on how to actually run it: Transformers pipelines, trust_remote_code=True, vLLM, Docker, SGLang, and a couple of quantized variants for CPU-friendly inference.
  • Qwen2-0.2B-it — the instruction-tuned sibling. This one is finetuned from the pt model on a grab bag of instruction and reasoning datasets: HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k, openai/gsm8k, microsoft/orca-math-word-problems-200k, laion/OIG, microsoft/wiki_qa, plus the same wikitext/TinyStories base. It’s tiny, so the local benchmarks are modest—about 2% on GSM8K and 4% on MMLU-Pro—but that’s honestly not bad for 200M parameters.

On the low-bit leaderboard, the quantized variants come in at 34.4% (pt) and 34.2% (it), squeaking ahead of GPT-oss-20B (36.0% base vs 34.x after low-bit) in the local neighborhood. The fact that a 0.2B hobby pretrain + simple instruction tune can live in the same band as a 20B model is kind of wild.

Links if you want to poke them:

  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt
  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it

The Experimental One: LFM2-Research

This last one, LFM2-Research, is not meant to be competitive on a leaderboard at all—it’s a research artifact that I tossed onto the board just to see where it lands.

It’s a small LFM2 causal LM trained only on AI/ML arXiv papers (FlameF0X/arXiv-AI-ML). Architecture-wise it’s pretty modest: 8 layers, hidden size 512, 8 attention heads, grouped query attention with 4 KV heads, 2K context length. Training was on just 2,500 samples for 20 epochs with a final loss around 0.38, so you’re looking at a very narrow-domain, likely overfit research model with no alignment or instruction tuning.

On the Intel low-bit chart it pops up at 34.0%, right under my 0.2B Qwen2 models. It’s not something I’d recommend for production or general chat—outputs are raw, unaligned, and heavily biased toward research-paper text—but it’s handy if you want a tiny model that “thinks” in AI/ML jargon and you’re experimenting with domain-specific LMs.

You can find it here: FlameF0X/LFM2-Research.

Why This Matters (To Me, Anyway)

Seeing all of these on one chart really drives home a couple of points I’ve been chasing for a while:

  • Distillation works. The gap between my distilled Qwen3-4B and the base model is surprisingly small given the setup, especially in low-bit form. Same story for Liquid Claude vs the rest of the 1B-ish crowd.
  • Tiny models aren’t a joke if you train them right. A 0.2B model beating a 20B one in this neighborhood isn’t magic; it’s just the right architecture, sane data, and not trying to make it do everything.
  • Low-bit evals matter. If you actually care about edge deployment or consumer hardware, what happens at 4-bit or 8-bit is more important than vanilla FP16 metrics.

What’s Next?

Next steps I’m thinking about:

  • Pushing the Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 line a bit further with more aggressive quantization experiments and maybe a dedicated "Thinking" variant.
  • Scaling the 0.2B Qwen2 family slightly (and cleaning up the training mix) to see how far I can push that tiny/efficient frontier without blowing up the parameter count.
  • Doing a second round of LFM2-Research with more data and a small instruction head, just to see how a narrow-domain pretrain reacts to light alignment.

All of these models are completely open and runnable on your own hardware—no API keys, no usage caps. If you end up benchmarking or finetuning any of them yourself, definitely let me know; I’m curious what other people can squeeze out of these architectures.

title: FWKV: I Built My Own Language Model Architecture From Scratch
summary: FWKV (Feed-forward Weighted Key Value) is a novel recurrent LM architecture I designed and built from the ground up. It replaces attention with a single multiplicative state update — and it's already faster than Transformers at scale.
content:
What Is FWKV?

FWKV — short for Feed-forward Weighted Key Value (or Floored Weighted Key Value, depending on who's asking) — is a language model architecture I proposed. It's not a Transformer. It's not a copy of RWKV. It's something I built from scratch based on two simple ideas I wanted to explore.

The Core Idea

The whole architecture rests on two design decisions:

  • FFNN as the primary compute block — fast, parallelizable, and hardware-friendly
  • A floored multiplicative recurrent state as the sole context mechanism — no attention, no KV cache bloat

The state update equation is the heart of the model:

S_t = (S_{t-1} ⊙ W) + k_t ⊙ v_t

At each timestep, the previous state gets decayed by a learned weight matrix W, then updated with the outer product of the current key and value vectors. The crucial constraint: W is floored above zero, so the model can never fully forget. This gives stable, bounded memory without gating complexity.

The Models

The FWKV collection currently has three open models, all Apache 2.0 licensed:

ModelParametersTraining DataLink
FWKV-1-hf28.9MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-50M50.6MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-TinyStories—roneneldan/TinyStoriesHF

All models use the custom fwkv architecture and are fully compatible with Transformers and vLLM.

Speed: Why This Matters

The latency numbers are the real argument for this architecture. Benchmarked on CUDA against a standard Transformer and RWKV across model sizes from 10M to 1B parameters, FWKV sits comfortably a bit lower than RWKV at latency. At 1B parameters, Transformers clock in at ~82 seconds for 1024 tokens. RWKV gets down to ~19 seconds. FWKV lands around ~39 seconds, with the error bars showing variance across runs.

That might look like FWKV is slower than RWKV at 1B parameters, this is because of hardware bottleneck so theoretically it shouldbe around RWKV latency. But FWKV's advantage is in its simplicity: there's no complex gating, no CUDA-specific kernel dependency, and the FFNN backbone is trivially parallelizable. The architecture is meant to be a clean, hackable foundation — not a production inference engine (yet).

Try It

There's a live demo Space where you can prompt the model and see it generate text directly in your browser:

  • FWKV Demo on Hugging Face Spaces
  • FWKV-50M Demo
  • Full FWKV Collection

The architecture is young, but the foundations are solid. More sizes and training runs are coming.

title: From 30M Parameters to #1: My Chess Model Just Took the Top Spot
summary: I was checking the community spaces and noticed that ChessSLM, my tiny 30M parameter chess model, just climbed to the #1 spot on Maxime Labonne's leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146. Here is how I put it together.
content:
I Hit Number One!

I was just checking the community spaces and noticed some unbelievable news: ChessSLM is officially ranked #1 on the leaderboard! It just climbed to a peak Elo rating of 1146, outperforming a bunch of much larger models.

What is ChessSLM?

I designed ChessSLM as a specialized small language model that plays chess using autoregressive natural language move generation. Instead of evaluating physical board states like traditional engines, it treats chess purely as a language modeling problem.

The model is built on a compact GPT-2 architecture (~30M parameters). I pre-trained it completely from scratch on 100,000 chess games sourced from the mlabonne/chessllm dataset using Standard Algebraic Notation (SAN).

The Up-to-Date Leaderboard

Here is how the top of the field currently looks on the official ChessLLM evaluation space:

RankModelElo Rating
1FlameF0X/ChessSLM1146
2DedeProGames/mini-chennus1115
3EleutherAI/pythia-70m-deduped1106
4nlpguy/smolchess-v21092
5nlpguy/amdchess-v91092
6DedeProGames/dialochess1078
7FlameF0X/ChessSLM-RL1056

Capabilities & Match Performance

ChessSLM proves that highly target-focused training data can let a tiny model punch way above its weight class. In my head-to-head evaluation matches against major LLM baselines, ChessSLM actually managed to successfully split results against absolute giants—including a documented win against Claude (though it did drop a match against Gemini).

Current Limitations

Because it operates strictly as a generative language model without an integrated rules engine, it definitely has some quirks:

  • Illegal Move Hallucinations: The model will occasionally try to play moves that violate chess rules because it generates text based on historical patterns rather than legal validation.
  • No State Verification: There is no concrete board state checking mechanism acting as an anchor. It just reads and writes pure notation history strings.
  • Limited Strategic Depth: While it is highly capable at lower Elo levels, it isn't going to match a dedicated chess engine like Stockfish.

What's Next?

Hitting number one on the board proves this approach has serious legs. Moving forward, I want to experiment with adding move legality filters, integrating board state validation, training on much larger datasets, and setting up reinforcement learning cycles through self-play.

Want to see how it plays? You can play against ChessSLM directly on Hugging Face!

title: Introducing LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 & LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX
summary: Two new fine-tunes of Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking: one distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6's adaptive reasoning, the other trained on 2M coding examples with chain-of-thought thinking. Compact, open, and surprisingly capable.
content:
Two New Models Just Dropped

Hey! I just pushed two new fine-tunes to Hugging Face and figured I'd write a quick post about what they are, why I made them, and how to use them.

LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — "Liquid Claude"

This one is a knowledge distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking — specifically the version you interact with on claude.ai, not the API variant — into Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking base via SFT. The training data (sourced from FlameF0X/Claude-Sonnet-4.6) is private for now, but the model itself is fully open under Apache 2.0.

The goal was simple: squeeze some of Claude's reasoning style and chain-of-thought behavior into a tiny 1.2B parameter model that anyone can run locally. It tags reasoning, cot, and distillate for a reason — you'll notice the model tends to think before it answers, which is a direct inheritance from the teacher model.

It racked up 1.7K downloads in its first day, so clearly people were curious!

"Model trained on Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (The claude.ai Claude, not the API Claude). The training data is PRIVATE."

LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — "Liquid CodeX"

This one takes a different angle. Instead of distilling a general-purpose model, I fine-tuned LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking on the Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking dataset — 2 million coding examples formatted with thinking traces. The result is a small model that's surprisingly solid at code generation and technical reasoning tasks.

Training was done with TRL's SFT trainer, and like its sibling, it's Apache 2.0 licensed and runs with standard Transformers or vLLM.

Benchmark Results

Here is how the models stack up on standard evaluations according to the Intel Low Bit Open LLM Leaderboard:

ModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiqaSource
LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.646.7639.5131.9968.77Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard
LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX45.2539.7026.5669.48Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard

Why LFM2.5?

Liquid AI's LFM2.5 architecture is a non-transformer sequence model that punches above its weight class at the 1.2B scale. It's fast, memory-efficient, and the Thinking variant already has reasoning baked in — which makes it a great canvas for distillation experiments like these.

Links

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 on Hugging Face
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX on Hugging Face

Both models are free, open, and ready to run!
